title: Entanglement cost in non-local quantum computation
abstract: This is a book-length treatment of the subject of non-local quantum computation (NLQC). NLQC is a method for implementing quantum operations that interact two systems without directly bringing the systems together. Instead, a single round of communication and shared entanglement is used. NLQC has appeared in the context of quantum cryptography, computational complexity, communication complexity, quantum gravity, and other applications. The understanding of entanglement cost in NLQC is closely tied to questions in all of these areas. We review upper and lower bounds on entanglement cost, as well as some of the applications of NLQC and its connections to other subjects.
summary: This paper provides an extensive review of non-local quantum computation, focusing on the amount of entanglement needed to perform operations between distant systems. It connects the entanglement cost of these operations to broader topics like quantum cryptography and quantum gravity.
main_problem: The paper investigates the entanglement cost required to implement non-local quantum computation (NLQC) using a single round of communication and shared entanglement.
main_result: The work provides a comprehensive review of the upper and lower bounds on the entanglement cost for NLQC and explores its connections to various fields.
method: The paper is a book-length theoretical treatment and review of the bounds and applications of non-local quantum computation.
model_or_system: The system consists of two spatially separated quantum systems that interact via a single round of classical communication and shared entanglement.
main_assumptions: The framework assumes a single round of communication and the availability of shared entanglement.
paper_structure: The paper serves as a book-length review, covering the definition of NLQC, bounds on entanglement cost, and its applications in cryptography, complexity, and quantum gravity.
